title: Tibetan Kangyur Editions of the Heart Sūtra
description: Comparison of the Heart Sūtra across major Kangyur editions. The Tibetan translation was made in the 8th-9th century from Sanskrit. Both short and long recensions exist in the Kangyur.

editions.authority: Standard reference edition used by Western scholars
editions.notes: The Degé is considered the most reliable edition due to careful editorial work. It serves as the base text for this critical edition.

editions.location: Beijing, commissioned by Qing Emperor Kangxi
editions.authority: Important alternate edition; differs from Degé in orthography and some readings
editions.notes: The Peking Kangyur was printed in Beijing for the Qing court. It preserves some readings different from Degé, occasionally reflecting older transmission.

editions.location: Narthang Monastery, near Shigatse, central Tibet
editions.notes: Closely related to the Lhasa edition; together they represent the central Tibetan textual tradition.

editions.authority: Latest major Kangyur edition; corrected from earlier editions
editions.notes: The Lhasa Kangyur was printed in the 20th century with corrections from earlier editions. It often agrees with Narthang against Degé.

editions.authority: Earliest printed Kangyur; important for establishing early readings
editions.notes: The Lithang was the first complete printed Kangyur. It sometimes preserves readings older than the more commonly used Degé.

editions.authority: One of the oldest surviving Kangyur manuscripts
editions.notes: The Stog Palace manuscript predates the printed editions and may preserve older readings. However, access is limited.

short_recension.note: The Tibetan tradition preserves both long and short recensions. The short recension lacks the frame narrative.
short_recension.location_in_kangyur: Sometimes included as a separate text or variant within the long recension entries
short_recension.relationship_to_long: The short recension corresponds more closely to T251 (Chinese) and the Hōryūji manuscript (Sanskrit). The long recension adds the frame narrative found in Sanskrit long recension and T257.

variant_readings.methodology: Key orthographic and substantive variants between Kangyur editions. Base text: Degé (D).

variants.note: Titles are identical across editions; 'Bhagavatī' (feminine) is notable

variants.issue: Orthographic variants in ཕུང་པོ་ལྔ (phung po lnga)
variants.note: Consistent use of ལྔ་པོ (lnga po, 'the five') across editions
All editions use zad pa (exhaustion/kṣaya) rather than a term for nirodha. This matches Sanskrit kṣaya and supports Nattier's back-translation thesis.
The Tibetan tradition includes oṃ in the mantra, unlike the earliest Chinese and Sanskrit witnesses. This reflects the long recension Sanskrit source.

variants.degé: ཟབ་མོ་སྣང་བ (zab mo snang ba, 'Illumination of the Profound')
variants.note: The samādhi name is consistent across Tibetan editions
Tibetan has 5 epithets (prajñāpāramitā mantra, great knowledge mantra, unsurpassed mantra, unequalled mantra, all-suffering-pacifying mantra), while Chinese T251 has 4 and T250 has 3.

translation_colophon.note: The colophon in most editions credits these translators and revisers. The translation was made during the Tibetan Empire period when major translation projects were sponsored by the court.

stemmatic_position.summary: The Tibetan translation was made from Sanskrit, not Chinese. It therefore represents a witness to the Sanskrit tradition, not an independent transmission line.
stemmatic_position.relationship_to_sanskrit: The Tibetan long recension corresponds to the Sanskrit long recension (with frame narrative). The Tibetan short recension corresponds to the Sanskrit short recension.
stemmatic_position.relationship_to_chinese: The Tibetan is a secondary witness for establishing the original Chinese text, mediated through Sanskrit.
value_for_text_criticism: Tibetan zad pa matches Sanskrit kṣaya, confirming back-translation from Chinese
Tibetan includes oṃ in mantra, reflecting Sanskrit long recension source
Tibetan preserves frame narrative matching Sanskrit long recension
Tibetan ma thob pa (no non-attainment) reflects Sanskrit na-aprāptiḥ interpolation

scholarly_refs: Silk, Jonathan A. (1994). 'The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions Contained in the Kanjur.' Wiener Studien zur Tibetologie und Buddhismuskunde 34.
84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha (2022). The Heart of the Perfection of Wisdom. https://read.84000.co/translation/toh21.html
Skilling, Peter (1994). Mahāsūtras: Great Discourses of the Buddha. Pali Text Society.
Eimer, Helmut (1992). Der Tantra-Teil des Kanjur. Arbeitskreis für Tibetische und Buddhistische Studien.
Harrison, Paul (1996). 'A Brief History of the Tibetan bKa' 'gyur.' In Tibetan Literature: Studies in Genre, pp. 70-94.
